_provenance.stage: Stage 1 — derived from live cards for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
_provenance.note: This Stage 1 JSON was reconstructed from the currently-published cards so external Stage 2 (Gemini bias) and Stage 3 (Codex fact-check) reviews can verify what readers see. It is not the original analysis — the original pipeline was not run on this issue.

headline: Fuel Quota Cut Hits April 1: Who Absorbs the 68% Price Jump
context: The monthly subsidised RON95 quota under BUDI95 drops from 300 to 200 litres on 1 April 2026. Government says 90% of users consume under 200 litres. Subsidised price stays at RM1.99/litre; above-quota purchases cost market rate. The subsidy bill has reached RM4 billion per month.

cards.text: RON95 stays at RM1.99. The government says 90% of users won't notice.
cards.sub: The quota drops from 300 to 200 litres per month on 1 April. The 90% figure has no published methodology, sample period, or source. It is a government assertion, not a verified statistic.

cards.h: Above-quota fuel costs 68% more. The burden falls on those who drive farthest with the fewest alternatives.
cards.s: Rural B40 commuters, plantation workers and gig riders have no public-transport fallback. T20 with larger cars benefit most.
cards.d: Above-quota fuel costs 68% more. The burden falls on those who drive farthest with the fewest alternatives. Rural B40 commuters, plantation workers and gig riders have no public-transport fallback. T20 with larger cars benefit most.

cards.h: Indonesia paired identical subsidy cuts with direct cash transfers. Malaysia offers no transitional support.
cards.s: India targeted LPG via direct transfers. Thailand stabilised diesel. Malaysia's cut arrives two days after Hari Raya price controls end. No cushion.
cards.d: Indonesia paired identical subsidy cuts with direct cash transfers. Malaysia offers no transitional support. India targeted LPG via direct transfers. Thailand stabilised diesel. Malaysia's cut arrives two days after Hari Raya price controls end. No cushion.

cards.h: Sixty years of car-centric planning left rural and East Malaysia with zero public transport. The quota cut punishes the result.
cards.s: Outside the Klang Valley: no buses, no trains, no alternatives. Women absorb shocks through trip rationing invisible in consumption data.
cards.d: Sixty years of car-centric planning left rural and East Malaysia with zero public transport. The quota cut punishes the result. Outside the Klang Valley: no buses, no trains, no alternatives. Women absorb shocks through trip rationing invisible in consumption data.

cards.text: The policy question is not whether 90% are unaffected. It is whether a country with no rural public transport can cut fuel access without first building alternatives.
Reform without compensation, alternatives or a published basis for the '90% unaffected' claim is not reform. It is cost-shifting to those with the least voice.

confidence: Low — derived from published cards, original brief not on disk; submit for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
